title: Listening - Part 4: Learning a Craft
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people describing their experience of taking up a traditional craft later in life

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d been an IT consultant for decades, and [21]I took up black-smithing because I was desperate for a hobby that produced something solid and real, rather than just code on a screen[/21]. I needed that physical connection. [26]The most rewarding aspect has been the boost in my patience and attention to detail[/26]. You can't rush iron. If you’re not focused, you ruin the piece. This new-found ability to slow down has actually made me much better at my day job, where I used to be much more impulsive and prone to mistakes. || Speaker 2: I’m a retired doctor, and [22]I started pottery because I wanted to rediscover a sense of play and creativity that I felt I’d lost during my medical career[/22]. I wanted to get messy. [27]What I found most difficult, though, was the frustration of how many of my early pieces simply collapsed or exploded in the kiln[/27]. I’m used to being a high achiever, so failing repeatedly was a hard but necessary lesson in humility. It’s taught me that the process is just as important as the final product. || Speaker 3: I’ve always been interested in history, so [23]I started learning traditional weaving primarily because I wanted to keep a dying local craft alive for future generations[/23]. I felt a sense of duty. [28]The most rewarding outcome has been the discovery of a small but passionate group of other weavers in my area[/28]. We meet once a week to share techniques and stories. It’s given me a social circle that is completely separate from my family life, and I value those friendships immensely. || Speaker 4: I’m a high-school teacher, and [24]I took up jewelry making because I wanted to find a way to earn a little extra income while doing something I actually enjoyed[/24]. I wanted a creative side-hustle. [29]The biggest hurdle has been the high cost of the specialized tools and materials like silver and gemstones[/29]. I’ve had to be very disciplined with my budget to keep the hobby going, which has been a stress I didn't anticipate. But seeing someone wear a piece I’ve made is an incredible feeling of pride. || Speaker 5: I’d recently gone through a stressful period at work, and [25]I started woodcarving because I needed a repetitive, meditative task that would quiet my mind[/25]. I needed to escape the noise. [30]The most unexpected benefit has been the improvement in my manual dexterity and hand-eye coordination[/30]. I’ve noticed that my hands feel much stronger and more precise in everything I do, from typing to cooking. It’s a physical benefit I hadn't even considered when I first picked up a chisel, and it’s made me feel much younger.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what primarily motivated each speaker to learn a craft.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 wanted to produce something solid and real, a physical connection away from code.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to rediscover a sense of play and creativity lost during her medical career.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to keep a dying local craft alive for future generations.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to earn extra income through a creative side-hustle.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 needed a repetitive, meditative task to quiet his mind after work stress.

options.D: to achieve a sense of tangible output
options.F: to prepare for a professional exhibition

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome of their craft.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions an improvement in his patience and attention to detail.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 found failing repeatedly with collapsed pieces to be a hard lesson in humility.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 highlights the social circle and friendships she found among other weavers.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 found the high cost of tools and materials like silver to be a significant hurdle.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 mentions an improvement in his manual dexterity and hand-eye coordination.

options.A: a realization that they have a business talent
options.B: a positive transfer of skills to their job
options.C: the pleasure of gifting their work to others
options.D: an improvement in a physical function
options.E: the discovery of a hidden creative flair
options.F: the financial burden of the hobby
options.G: struggling with a lack of initial proficiency
options.H: the value of the social group they joined
